content:
Hi,
I'm looking at the kernel source code. I couldn't see any Rust wrapper for struct file_operations, except in rust/bindings/.
I also noticed that in rust/kernel/drm/, it's used used from the bindings directly. Same thing with rust/kernel/miscdevice.rs.
Is there any plan to create a wrapper for them?
Although it could make sense to have shared logic, there is no such logic yet.
Apparently, there is, in the wrapper for Miscellaneous Devices rust/kernel/miscdevice.rs, in trait MiscDevice

Hi @Alice Ryhl,

In rust/kernel/miscdevice.rs, why have you named the trait for struct file_operations as MiscDevice?
Why not name it FileOperations, and its implementations (misc, char) can have the specific name, MiscdeviceVTable in our case.

I also wonder if the implementations for different device types are any different from each other because, it's just a Rust-safe wrapping of struct file_operations, that is used by all device types equally.

Yes some methods in struct file_operations are specifically made for block devices, and no one implements them in their misc device, but there are general methods like open() and release().
I named the trait MiscDevice because it was designed to be useful for miscdevice without considering any other use-case.
Having a general trait would be great, but that's not what I attemped to make with MiscDevice

@Alice Ryhl In rust/kernel/uaccess.rs, in read_raw(), in len > self.length, why does the function return an error if the kernel buffer is larger than the user buffer? Shouldn't it be the opposite?

Also, in let res = unsafe { bindings::copy_from_user(out_ptr, self.ptr.as_const_ptr(), len) };, shouldn't len be self.length? The kernel only wants to read the amount that the user provided

Also, in self.length -= len;, why does it subtract the kernel buffer size from the user buffer? Shouldn't it just make self.length = 0, because it read everything?

Here's what I mean:

pub fn read_raw(&mut self, out: &mut [MaybeUninit<u8>]) -> Result {
        let len = out.len();
        let out_ptr = out.as_mut_ptr().cast::<c_void>();
        if self.length > len {
            return Err(EFAULT);
        }
        // SAFETY: `out_ptr` points into a mutable slice of length `len`, so we may write
        // that many bytes to it.
        let res =unsafe { bindings::copy_from_user(out_ptr, self.ptr.as_const_ptr(), self.length) };
        if res != 0 {
            return Err(EFAULT);
        }
        self.ptr = self.ptr.wrapping_byte_add(len);
        self.length = 0;
        Ok(())
    }
The way it works was chosen because that's what is most convenient for the actual use-case I had for the function.

So I can't use any of read_raw, read_slice, read_all if my usecase has a kernel buffer that is larger than the user buffer?
If so, should I add a different method for this usecase?
besides, you can create a shorter slice of your kernel buffer based on the length

Alice Ryhl said:

besides, you can create a shorter slice of your kernel buffer based on the length

Fair

In samples/rust/rust_misc_device.rs. You used inner as a variable that gets destroyed when the file closes. Is there a way to make it device-wide or module-wide? So even when closing the file, it retains its data.

One way is to create a global variable (static mut) like in C, but is there any other way that can be contained in the module's or the device's struct maybe?
